app.name: Claude Code
app.bestFor: Terminal-first agentic coding on large repos; Reddit's top pick for hard repository work, with the deepest plugin/skills ecosystem.
app.rankBasis: The consensus #1 for hard repository work across the three cited 2026 comparisons, with especially strong Reddit preference and the deepest first-party skills/plugin ecosystem among terminal agents.
app.pricingFree: Free Claude plan includes limited Claude Code access on the web
app.pricingPaid: Claude Pro $20/mo ($17/mo billed annually) - Claude Code in terminal and web with higher usage limits
features: tool use, tests and Git workflows
pros: strongest consensus for difficult multi-file repository work
excellent instruction following and long-context reasoning
large and fast-growing skills/plugin ecosystem
cons: usage limits can feel tight on long agent sessions
terminal-first workflow is less approachable than an IDE
autonomous edits still require careful review and testing

app.name: Cursor
app.bestFor: The gold-standard AI IDE — most broadly adopted for daily editor-led development among individual devs and small teams.
app.rankBasis: Consistently placed in the top two across all three cited 2026 comparisons and remains the most broadly adopted AI-native editor for everyday individual and small-team development.
app.pricingFree: Hobby: limited Agent requests and limited Tab completions, no card required
app.pricingPaid: Pro $20/mo ($16/mo billed annually) - extended Agent limits, frontier models, skills, hooks and cloud agents
features: cloud agents, skills and MCP support
pros: polished editor experience with very low workflow friction
fast codebase indexing and context-aware completions
broad model choice and mature agent features
cons: included model usage can be difficult to predict
power users often need higher-priced Pro+ or usage billing
large autonomous changes still need close developer review

app.name: GitHub Copilot
app.bestFor: Safest enterprise default with the broadest editor coverage and native GitHub workflow integration.
app.rankBasis: A top-three pick in every cited 2026 comparison, backed by GitHub's distribution, the broadest IDE coverage and the strongest native integration with repositories, pull requests and enterprise policy.
app.pricingFree: Copilot Free: 2,000 completions and 50 chat/agent requests per month
app.pricingPaid: Copilot Pro $10/mo - unlimited completions, coding agent and 300 premium requests/mo
features: GitHub coding agent and pull requests
pros: broadest editor and GitHub workflow integration
lowest-cost mainstream paid coding assistant
strong enterprise administration and policy controls
cons: agent quality can trail specialist tools on complex repo work
premium-request accounting adds usage complexity
generated code can contain bugs or insecure patterns and needs review

app.name: OpenAI Codex
app.bestFor: The strongest GPT-native agent (CLI + cloud), good for delegated tasks with auto-test loops.
app.rankBasis: A top CLI/cloud-agent recommendation across all three cited 2026 roundups, differentiated by one account spanning local Codex, the desktop app and delegated cloud tasks with isolated worktrees.
app.pricingFree: Limited Codex access is available on ChatGPT Free and Go during the current introductory period
app.pricingPaid: ChatGPT Plus $20/mo - Codex CLI/app/cloud tasks with plan-based usage limits
features: local CLI and desktop coding agent
tool execution, tests and code review
pros: strong integration between local work and cloud delegation
effective test-run-fix loops for scoped engineering tasks
good Git and multi-task workflow ergonomics
cons: plan limits vary with model and task complexity
cloud tasks require repository access and careful permissions
agent output still needs human architectural judgment

app.name: Windsurf (Devin Desktop)
app.bestFor: Clean agent-UX editor at a lower price point; rebranded under Devin/Cognition in mid-2026.
app.rankBasis: Still appears in all three cited editor comparisons and now benefits from Cognition's unified Devin Desktop/Cloud product; its clean agent UX remains the main alternative to Cursor.
app.pricingFree: Free: light agent quota, limited models, unlimited inline edits and Tab completions
app.pricingPaid: Devin Pro $20/mo - higher quotas, frontier models and access to Devin Cloud agents
pros: clean, approachable agent-oriented editor UX
unified local editor and cloud-agent workflow
useful free tier for lightweight daily coding
cons: product naming and ownership changes have caused user uncertainty
paid usage allowances are described as quotas rather than fixed requests
smaller extension and community ecosystem than Cursor or VS Code

app.name: Devin (Cognition)
app.bestFor: The purest autonomous agent — delegate well-scoped tasks to parallel cloud VMs that open PRs when done.
app.rankBasis: The autonomy specialist across all three cited 2026 comparisons: unlike editor copilots, Devin is repeatedly selected for parallel cloud tasks that work through repositories and return pull requests.
app.pricingFree: Free: light agent quota, limited models, unlimited inline edits and Tab completions
app.pricingPaid: Devin Pro $20/mo - increased quotas, frontier models and Devin Cloud access; extra usage at API pricing
pros: strongest product focus on asynchronous task delegation
isolated cloud sessions can run multiple tasks in parallel
integrates with Git providers and issue trackers
cons: works best only when tasks are tightly scoped and well specified
long autonomous runs can consume substantial usage
reviewing an agent-produced pull request can offset time saved

app.name: Gemini CLI
app.bestFor: Strong large-repo ingestion with the most generous free tier; a solid no-cost CLI agent entry point.
app.rankBasis: Included by both cited CLI-agent roundups and distinguished by Google's unusually generous individual quota plus a very large context window; independent adoption is newer than the leaders above it.
app.pricingFree: Free with a personal Google account: up to 60 requests/min and 1,000 requests/day
app.pricingPaid: Google AI Pro $19.99/mo - higher Gemini limits; API pay-as-you-go also available
pros: most generous no-cost quota among major CLI agents
large context window suits broad repository ingestion
open-source and easy to extend with tools
cons: agent behavior is less mature than Claude Code or Codex
quality varies substantially by task and selected model
Google account and cloud-service dependencies reduce local privacy

skill.name: Superpowers
skill.bestFor: The recognized leader of the Claude Code skills ecosystem (~260k★): composable SKILL.md skills chaining brainstorming, planning, subagent-driven TDD and code review.
skill.rankBasis: ~260.4k GitHub stars make Superpowers the most-adopted coding-skill framework by a wide margin; it is also the first recommendation in the cited 2026 Claude Code skills roundup.
pros: opinionated end-to-end engineering process rather than isolated prompts
very large community and broad agent compatibility
strong emphasis on tests, review and incremental execution
cons: rigid workflow can feel heavy for small or exploratory tasks
many coordinated skills increase context and execution time
quality still depends on the host model following the process

skill.name: OpenCode
skill.bestFor: The most-starred open-source coding agent (~189k★): terminal-native, editor-agnostic, 75+ model providers — the leading OSS alternative to Claude Code.
skill.rankBasis: ~189.2k GitHub stars make OpenCode the most-starred open-source coding agent; it leads the cited OSS ranking and has exceptional adoption for a provider-neutral terminal tool.
skill.pricingFree: Free & open-source (self-host; bring your own model credentials)
pros: provider-neutral alternative to proprietary coding agents
fast terminal UI with a very large community
open architecture supports local and hosted models
cons: model API usage is paid separately
rapid development can introduce breaking changes
users must manage providers, permissions and configuration

skill.name: Anthropic Skills (official)
skill.bestFor: Anthropic's official SKILL.md skill library (~164k★) — production-grade skills like webapp testing, MCP-server building and document editing that any agent can load.
skill.rankBasis: ~163.9k GitHub stars plus Anthropic's first-party ownership make this the canonical Agent Skills reference library and the strongest source for production-grade SKILL.md examples.
pros: canonical examples maintained by the creator of the skills format
high-quality production-oriented instructions and resources
useful as both runnable skills and authoring references
cons: collection is selective rather than a comprehensive registry
some skills require extra runtimes or external tools
best integration remains within Anthropic's agent products

skill.name: awesome-claude-skills
skill.bestFor: The most-starred curated directory of Agent Skills (~70k★): one place to discover and install community SKILL.md skills for coding and beyond.
skill.rankBasis: ~69.8k GitHub stars make it the largest curated Claude-skills directory, but it ranks below executable frameworks because adoption measures the list itself rather than any individual skill's quality.
pros: broad discovery surface for the fragmented skills ecosystem
easy way to compare many community projects
actively followed and frequently updated
cons: curation does not guarantee each linked skill is safe or maintained
quality and installation conventions vary widely
the directory itself does not execute or validate skills

skill.name: Cline
skill.bestFor: The most widely installed open-source coding agent (~65k★, 5M+ installs): an autonomous plan/act agent in VS Code with human-in-the-loop control.
skill.rankBasis: ~65k GitHub stars and more than five million reported installs make Cline the most widely deployed open-source IDE coding agent in this list; it is a top pick in the cited OSS roundup.
skill.pricingFree: Free & open-source extension/CLI (bring your own model credentials)
features: VS Code extension, CLI and SDK
human approval for file and command actions
pros: mature human-in-the-loop controls for autonomous changes
large install base and active extension ecosystem
works with many hosted and local model providers
cons: model API costs can grow quickly during long tasks
approval-heavy flows can become tedious
autonomous loops can consume large context windows

skill.name: goose
skill.bestFor: Block's open-source on-machine agent (~52k★): a general-purpose local coding agent that runs tasks end-to-end via any LLM and MCP extensions.
skill.rankBasis: ~51.6k GitHub stars, Block stewardship and top placement in the cited open-source CLI roundup establish goose as a leading extensible on-machine agent.
skill.pricingFree: Free & open-source (self-host; bring your own model credentials)
pros: runs locally while remaining model-provider neutral
strong extensibility through MCP tools
backed by an experienced production engineering organization
cons: provider setup and API billing remain the user's responsibility
general-purpose design is less coding-specialized than some rivals
tool permissions require careful configuration

skill.name: Aider
skill.bestFor: The most mature, battle-tested terminal pair-programmer (~47k★, 4M+ installs): deep Git integration with auto-commits.
skill.rankBasis: ~47.7k GitHub stars and more than four million reported installs give Aider the longest battle-tested record among terminal pair-programmers; it remains near the top of star-based OSS rankings.
skill.pricingFree: Free & open-source (self-host; bring your own model credentials)
pros: mature Git-native workflow with inspectable commits
works across a very broad range of models
efficient repository maps reduce unnecessary context
cons: terminal UX has a real learning curve
API costs and quality depend on the chosen model
less autonomous task management than newer agent platforms

skill.name: Continue
skill.bestFor: Popular open-source autopilot (~35k★) for VS Code and JetBrains, letting teams build and share custom AI code assistants and rules.
skill.rankBasis: ~35.1k GitHub stars and placement in the cited OSS ranking make Continue the leading open-source option for teams that want configurable assistants inside both VS Code and JetBrains.
skill.pricingFree: Free & open-source core (self-host; bring your own model credentials)
pros: strong cross-editor support including JetBrains
highly configurable for private models and team rules
open-source foundation avoids single-provider lock-in
cons: setup is more involved than turnkey commercial assistants
experience quality varies with model and configuration
team deployment and model hosting can require real operations work

skill.name: Vercel Agent Skills
skill.bestFor: Vercel's official SKILL.md collection (~29k★): React/Next.js performance and composition best-practices plus project auditing and instant deploys.
skill.rankBasis: ~29.4k GitHub stars and Vercel's first-party authorship make this the most-adopted framework-specific skill collection, with unusually concrete React and Next.js engineering guidance.
pros: first-party, framework-specific recommendations
codifies detailed performance and composition practices
useful for both implementation and automated review
cons: narrowly optimized for the React, Next.js and Vercel ecosystem
framework guidance can age as platform conventions change
skills provide instructions rather than deterministic enforcement

skill.name: agentskills.io (open standard + registry)
skill.bestFor: The open Agent Skills spec and directory (~23k★) — discover SKILL.md skills that work across Claude Code, Codex, Gemini CLI, Cursor and more.
skill.rankBasis: ~23.4k GitHub stars plus support across Claude Code, Codex, Gemini CLI and Cursor make agentskills.io the central open specification rather than another vendor-specific skill list.
pros: reduces lock-in through a simple portable format
supported by several major coding-agent ecosystems
clear reference material for skill authors
cons: compatibility details still differ between host agents
the open format does not guarantee skill quality or security
registry coverage is smaller than broad community lists

skill.name: Trail of Bits Skills
skill.bestFor: Security-focused SKILL.md skills from a top audit firm (~6k★) — CodeQL and Semgrep vulnerability detection wired into the agent's workflow. Niche but high-signal.
skill.rankBasis: ~6.2k GitHub stars are modest beside general coding tools, but Trail of Bits' security-audit reputation and specialized CodeQL/Semgrep workflows give this collection unusually high signal in its niche.
pros: authored by a respected security research and audit firm
encodes concrete specialist workflows rather than generic prompts
valuable for repeatable vulnerability-analysis tasks
cons: niche collection rather than a general coding toolkit
effective use requires security-domain expertise
some workflows depend on heavyweight external analysis tools

notes: Rich, high-consensus category with a genuine Agent Skill ecosystem. App track reflects settled 2026 developer consensus (Claude Code / Cursor / Copilot lead; Devin/Windsurf for autonomy). The skill track now ranks genuine SKILL.md skills AND notable open-source coding-agent repos TOGETHER by real adoption/reputation (GitHub stars), with kind ('skill'|'repo') as a tag only — NOT a rank determinant. This fixes the prior bug where all skills were forced above all repos regardless of quality (e.g. Trail of Bits ranked above agentskills.io): now Superpowers (~260k★ skill) leads, but hugely-adopted repos like OpenCode (~189k★) and Cline (~65k★) correctly outrank smaller skills, and agentskills.io (~23k★, high) sits above Trail of Bits (~6k★, medium). Confidence is consistent with rank (high→medium). Roo Code was excluded as its main repo is archived; Kilo Code is a live alternative not shown to keep the list focused. Previous MCP-server entries (Context7, GitHub, Playwright MCP) remain out of scope for this track.
